title: Salesforce OAuth2 keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Salesforce OAuth2 consumer key (client ID) detected. Salesforce consumer keys are used to identify connected apps in OAuth2 authentication flows for accessing Salesforce APIs, data, and services. These identifiers are part of the OAuth2/OIDC authentication process and when exposed alongside consumer secrets, enable unauthorized access to Salesforce resources including CRM data, custom objects, metadata, and platform services. While consumer keys alone are not sufficient for authentication, exposing them reveals application architecture and OAuth2 configuration details that can be leveraged by attackers. Consumer keys should be stored as environment variables or in configuration management systems, never hardcoded in source code. Review connected app configuration in Salesforce Setup and implement secure credential management practices including IP restrictions, OAuth policies, and regular credential rotation.

Salesforce OAuth2 consumer secret (client secret) detected - 64-character hexadecimal format. Salesforce consumer secrets are confidential credentials used by connected apps to authenticate with Salesforce OAuth2 token endpoints. These secrets are the cryptographic keys that prove an application's identity during OAuth2 authorization code exchange, JWT bearer flow, username-password flow, and token refresh operations. Exposed consumer secrets allow attackers to: impersonate legitimate connected apps, obtain access tokens for Salesforce APIs, access CRM data including accounts/contacts/opportunities without authorization, modify Salesforce metadata and configurations, extract sensitive business data, bypass OAuth2 security controls, compromise single sign-on sessions, execute unauthorized API calls, and escalate privileges within Salesforce organizations. This represents a critical security vulnerability in Salesforce integration infrastructure. Immediately revoke the exposed consumer secret through Salesforce Setup > App Manager > Connected App settings, generate new credentials with appropriate OAuth scopes, update all application configurations, audit API usage logs for unauthorized access, implement IP restrictions and OAuth policies, and use secure secrets management with HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. Never commit consumer secrets to version control or expose in client-side code.

Salesforce OAuth2 consumer secret (client secret) detected - alphanumeric format. Salesforce consumer secrets are confidential credentials used by connected apps to authenticate with Salesforce OAuth2 token endpoints. These secrets are the cryptographic keys that prove an application's identity during OAuth2 authorization code exchange, JWT bearer flow, web server flow, and token refresh operations. Exposed consumer secrets allow attackers to: impersonate legitimate connected apps, obtain access tokens for all Salesforce APIs (REST, SOAP, Bulk, Streaming), access sensitive CRM data including accounts/contacts/leads/opportunities/cases, query and extract business-critical information, modify Salesforce records and configurations, execute SOQL/SOSL queries to discover data structure, deploy malicious Apex code or triggers, compromise Lightning applications, bypass OAuth2 security controls and IP restrictions, steal customer PII and financial data, manipulate sales pipelines and forecasts, and escalate privileges within multi-tenant Salesforce organizations. This represents a critical security vulnerability affecting all Salesforce integrations. Immediately revoke the exposed consumer secret through Salesforce Setup > App Manager > [Connected App Name] > Manage Consumer Details, generate new credentials with minimum required OAuth scopes (avoid full access), update all application configurations including mobile apps and integration middleware, audit API usage through Event Monitoring or Setup Audit Trail for unauthorized access patterns, implement IP restrictions via connected app OAuth policies, enable OAuth token encryption, require high-assurance sessions, use certificate-based authentication where possible, store secrets in enterprise-grade secret managers (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, CyberArk), implement oauth token lifecycle management, enable two-factor authentication for API access, monitor for unusual API call volumes, and rotate credentials every 90 days. Never commit consumer secrets to version control, expose in client-side JavaScript, log in application logs, or share across environments.
